content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration · · · Executive Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                322.5901              322.6441              322.6444

December 27, 1977

Mr. R. L. Yeomans
Assistant City Attorney
City of Oceanside
Civic Center
321 North Nevada
Oceanside, CA 92054

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 77-040

Dear Mr. Yeomans:

     Thank you for your letter of October 20, 1977, requesting a formal opinion with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The Commission will not render a formal opinion on the matter because no substantial questions of interpretation of the Political Reform Act are raised. However, I trust the following advice issued pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) will enable you to resolve the matter.

     The facts as we understand them are as follows. Pursuant to Public Resources Code Section 21000, et seq., the City Planning Commission is obliged to consider the adverse environmental impacts of a wide variety of projects coming before that body for approval. If the Planning Commission finds that a significant impact exists, the California Environmental Quality Act of 1974 mandates that the project be denied unless certain countervailing circumstances are present.

     The environmental issue referred to above is but one of several considerations entered into by the Planning Commission in approving or rejecting a proposed project. However, it is, generally speaking, a threshold issue. Because of the number and complexity of projects coming before the Planning Commission, the City Council of Oceanside

     1/ As a practical matter, no project in the City of Oceanside has been approved where such adverse environmental impact was found to exist.

Mr. R. L. Yeomans
December 27, 1977
Page Two


has established a separate public agency to screen projects
which raise the environmental impact issue. If it is determined
by this screening body that such impact may exist, the applicant
then retains a private consultant for the purpose of preparing
an environmental impact report for presentation to the Planning
Commission. If the screening body does not find the potential
for an adverse impact, the project is forwarded directly to
the Planning Commission for consideration on other issues.
The screening body is not composed of nor controlled by
members of the Planning Commission.

     In preparing an environmental impact report, the
private consultant is working pursuant to a contractual
agreement with the applicant. While environmental impact
reports are supposed to contain independent and objective
analysis, the fact that the permit applicant selects and
pays the preparer of the impact report creates a situation
where the preparer may slant his environmental analysis to
assure future business from the applicant. Neither the
Planning Commission nor any other agency of the City prepares
any independent analysis of the environmental impact of a
project. Accordingly, the Planning Commission frequently
relies heavily on the facts supplied and conclusions reached
by the private consultant concerning the matter. The Planning
Commission considers the environmental issue as a threshold
matter and if it concludes that an adverse impact will not
result from approval of the project, it will then proceed to
consider the other issues concerning the project.

     In the instant case, the spouse of one of the
Planning Commissioners is a salaried full-time employee of
an environmental consulting firm which prepares reports of
the type discussed above for consideration by the Planning
Commission.2/ The spouse evaluates the biological impact of
a project and his conclusions are a part of the total environmental impact report.

     The question thus presented is: In light of the
provisions of Government Code Section 87100, when must the
Planning Commissioner in question refrain from voting, or

_______________________________
2/ The spouse does not, however, have an ownership
interest in the firm.

Mr. R. L. Yeomans
December 27, 1977
Page Three


otherwise participating in the consideration of certain
matters coming before the Commission?-3/

The environmental consulting firm is a "source of
income" to the Planning Commissioner within the meaning of
Government Code Section 87103(c) by virtue of the Commissioner's
community property interest in the income of her spouse.
See Government Code Section 82030. Accordingly, she must
disqualify herself from making or participating in the making
of any decision that will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the consulting firm.

As a general rule, we think that the Planning
Commission's actions in approving or disapproving environ-
mental impact reports will have foreseeable repercussive
effects on the consulting firms preparing such reports.
Obviously, future applicants coming before the Planning
Commission will strive to enlist the services of a firm
which has a "track record" of success before the Commission.
As you note in your letter, it is likely that applicants may
favor the firm for whom the spouse of the Planning Commissioner

3/ Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 provide,
in relevant part:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.
                                                Gov. Code §87100

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reason-
ably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on:
                                                ***

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is
made;

                                                Gov. Code §87103(c)

Mr. R. L. Yeomans
December 27, 1977
Page Four


works because of a perceived, if not real, advantage that
firm may have before the Commission if the spouse were to
participate in such matters.

We also think that in most circumstances this
effect will be "material" within the meaning of Government
Code Section 87103. The Commission has issued regulations
defining "material financial effect," 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(a), which provides in relevant part:

     The financial effect of a governmental decision on
     a financial interest of a public official is material
     if, at the time the official makes, participates
     in making or attempts to use his or her official
     position to influence the making of the decision
     in light of all the circumstances and facts known
     at the time of the decision, the official knows or
     has reason to know that the existence of the financial
     interest might interfere with the official's performance
     of his or her duties in an impartial manner free
     from bias.

In light of all the circumstances surrounding the
instant case, we think a reasonable person would conclude
that the Planning Commissioner might not be impartial in
evaluating reports submitted by her husband or her husband's
firm. In a case such as this, the Planning Commissioner may
be biased in her judgment concerning a project's environmental
impact because of the financial interest of her husband and
her husband's firm in seeing the project approved on environ-
mental grounds. Lastly, the decision clearly will affect
the firm in question in a manner different from its effect
on the public generally.

     Accordingly, we believe that Government Code Sections
87100 and 87103 require the Planning Commission to refrain
from making or participating in any decisions of the Commission
concerning the existence of adverse environmental impact of
projects where the consulting firm in question has prepared
the environmental impact report. The Commissioner is not
disqualified from participating in any decisions concerning
those projects which raise issues unrelated to the threshold
environmental issue.

Mr. R. L. Yeomans
December 27, 1977
Page Five


     I trust this answers your question.  If you wish
to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so
pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have any
further questions, please contact Kenneth Goshorn of the
Conflict of Interests Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission at (916) 322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:KG:plh

CITY OF OCEANSIDE

October 20, 1977

Legal Department
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 307
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Conflict of Interest Opinion

Dear Staff:

In accordance with the Government Code, the City of Oceanside has created and maintains a Planning Commission which finally approves or denies variances and conditional use permits, and renders advisory opinion to the City Council concerning zone changes, specific plans, development plans, and tentative subdivision maps.

The spouse of one of the Planning Commissioners is a salaried, full time employee of an environmental consulting firm which is utilized by some developers appearing before the Oceanside Planning Commission, to analyze the environmental impacts of their projects. The spouse evaluates the biological impacts of projects and his conclusion regarding the presence or absence of a significant adverse environmental impact becomes a part of the firm's analysis. The analyses of environmental consulting firms are always considered, and are often relied upon by members of the Commission in forming their decision as to whether a project causes significant adverse environmental impacts. If the Commission finds that there are such impacts, the California Environmental Quality Act of 1974 mandate that a project be denied, unless certain countervailing circumstances exist.

It is probable that upon occasion in the future the Planning Commissioner will be voting to approve a project, the environmental analysis of which (a) was prepared by the environmental consulting firm employing the Commissioner's spouse; and (b) contains the environmental conclusion of the Commissioner's spouse.

CIVIC CENTER • [illegible] • CALIFORNIA • [illegible] • [illegible]

Legal Department
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 20, 1977
Page 2


     In addition, it is conceivable that applicants appear-
ing before the Commission may elect to have the environmen-
tal analysis of their project prepared by the firm employ-
ing the spouse, because of a perceived advantage based on
the relationship of the firm's employee and the Planning
Commission.

     This office respectfully requests an opinion as to
whether the foregoing facts and possibilities raise a con-
flict of interest, as defined by the California Political
Reform Act of 1974 which would bar the Planning Commissioner
from voting on certain projects. If there are further ques-
tions, or if additional information is needed, please call
me at (714) 722-2278 or 433-9000, ext. 308

                                Very truly yours,

                                RONALD E. MUHL
                                City Attorney


                                By [illegible]
                                R. L. YEOMANS
                                Assistant City Attorney

sections.question: In light of the provisions of Government Code Section 87100, when must the Planning Commissioner in question refrain from voting, or otherwise participating in the consideration of certain matters coming before the Commission?
sections.conclusion: Accordingly, we believe that Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 require the Planning Commission to refrain from making or participating in any decisions of the Commission concerning the existence of adverse environmental impact of projects where the consulting firm in question has prepared the environmental impact report. The Commissioner is not disqualified from participating in any decisions concerning those projects which raise issues unrelated to the threshold environmental issue.

sections.question_synthetic: When must a Planning Commissioner recuse herself from voting on environmental impact reports prepared by an environmental consulting firm where the Commissioner's spouse is a salaried employee of that firm, given Government Code Section 87100 conflict of interest provisions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Planning Commissioner must recuse herself from decisions concerning the environmental impact analysis of projects where her spouse's consulting firm prepared the environmental impact report, as the financial interest of her spouse in project approval would create a material financial effect and potential bias under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. However, she may participate in decisions on other non-environmental issues related to those projects.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document has OCR degradation but question and conclusion sections are clearly identifiable. Document declined to issue formal opinion but provided advice under Government Code Section 3114(b). Some text fragmentation in OCR version did not prevent accurate extraction of key sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In light of the provisions of Government Code Section 87100, when must the Planning Commissioner in question refrain from voting, or otherwise participating in the consideration of certain matters coming before the Commission?

CONCLUSION: Accordingly, we believe that Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 require the Planning Commission to refrain from making or participating in any decisions of the Commission concerning the existence of adverse environmental impact of projects where the consulting firm in question has prepared the environmental impact report. The Commissioner is not disqualified from participating in any decisions concerning those projects which raise issues unrelated to the threshold environmental issue.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the City of Oceanside that a Planning Commissioner whose spouse is a salaried employee of an environmental consulting firm that prepares impact reports for projects before the Commission must recuse herself from decisions on the environmental impact portion of those projects due to conflicts of interest under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.
